prompts.title: Background, Identity, Interest, or Talent
prompts.prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
tips: Choose a specific story or moment rather than listing qualities — show, don't tell
Focus on HOW this aspect shaped you, not just what it is
Connect your background/identity to your future goals or how you'll contribute
Avoid trauma dumping; keep emotional stakes proportionate to the space
pitfalls: Using the essay to list achievements instead of revealing character
Choosing an identity marker you've never reflected on before
Writing a victim narrative that leaves no agency for the applicant
examples: The smell of my grandmother's sofrito still transports me to her cramped kitchen in Queens, where I first understood that cooking was chemistry with soul.

prompts.title: Lessons from Obstacles
prompts.prompt: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?
tips: Start in the middle of the action — avoid lengthy setup
Spend more space on reflection and growth than on describing the failure itself
Be specific about what you learned and how you applied it later
Show (don't tell) the change through concrete behavior, not abstract claims
pitfalls: Spending 80% of the essay describing the problem and only 20% on growth
Choosing a failure that's actually a humble brag in disguise
Claiming you learned something generic like 'hard work pays off'
examples: I stared at the red F scrawled across my organic chemistry midterm and felt my medical school dreams hemorrhage onto the page.

prompts.title: Challenging a Belief or Idea
prompts.prompt: Reflect on a time when you challenged a belief or idea. What prompted you to act? Would you make the same decision again?
tips: Choose a belief that genuinely mattered to you or your community
Show the internal conflict before you acted — what held you back initially?
Address the counterargument honestly rather than dismissing it
Conclude with genuine reflection, including any nuance you'd add today
pitfalls: Picking a belief you were already confident challenging (no real stakes)
Portraying yourself as a lone crusader without acknowledging complexity
Being vague about what the belief was or why it mattered

prompts.title: Gratitude and Inspiration
prompts.prompt: Reflect on something that someone has done for you that has made you happy or thankful in a surprising way. How has this gratitude affected or inspired you?
tips: The 'surprising' element is key — choose an unexpected kindness, not a predictable one
Connect gratitude to action: how did this person change your behavior or trajectory?
Avoid sycophancy — don't just praise the person, use them as a mirror for yourself
Make sure the person you write about would recognize themselves in your essay
pitfalls: Writing a thank-you letter instead of a self-reflective essay
Choosing a parent or close family member (too expected, often too generic)
Over-focusing on the giver instead of your own transformation

prompts.title: Accomplishment and Personal Growth
prompts.prompt: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
tips: The 'realization' pivot is crucial — what did you suddenly understand?
Show the before/after you clearly, but let the reader see the transformation
Connect personal growth to understanding OTHERS, not just yourself
Choose something genuinely meaningful, not a résumé achievement
pitfalls: Writing about an award or accolade without the internal shift
Being too vague about what the 'realization' actually was
Confusing external accomplishment with internal change

prompts.title: Engaging Topic, Idea, or Concept
prompts.prompt: Describe a topic, idea, or concept you find so engaging that it makes you lose track of time. Why does it captivate you? What or who do you turn to when you want to learn more?
tips: Show your enthusiasm through specific details, not generic excitement
Explain WHY it matters to you, not just what fascinates you
Include what you DO with this interest — reading, creating, exploring
Make it specific enough that an expert would recognize your genuine engagement
pitfalls: Choosing something 'impressive' you think admissions wants to see
Listing facts instead of showing genuine intellectual passion
Being so broad (e.g., 'science') that you reveal nothing unique
examples: I have a recurring nightmare where I'm trapped in an infinite hotel corridor. It started after I first learned about Hilbert's Hotel, and now I can't stop thinking about what happens when you try to check in an infinite number of guests.

prompts.title: Topic of Your Choice
prompts.prompt: Share an essay on any topic you want. It can be one you already wrote, one that responds to a different prompt, or one of your own design.
tips: Use this freedom wisely — a random topic wastes the opportunity
The best open-topic essays reveal something the other prompts wouldn't capture
Still follow strong essay mechanics: specific story, meaningful reflection
Avoid choosing this option because you're 'not sure what else to write'
pitfalls: Submitting a school assignment or research paper excerpt
Using this to rehash your activities list in prose form
Being deliberately provocative for attention's sake

prompts.title: Harvard Community Contribution
prompts.prompt: Harvard has more than 500 student organizations and groups. How will you contribute to the Harvard community? (For reference: what kind of community do you want to find, and what will you give back?)
tips: Name specific organizations or traditions, not just generic categories
Show what you would DO, not just who you would meet
Connect your past contributions to what you'll bring to Harvard
Be honest about what you want to find, not just what you offer
pitfalls: Listing a string of clubs without showing genuine connection
Saying you want to 'meet people from different backgrounds' (too generic)
Ignoring the reciprocal nature — what you'll gain AND give

prompts.title: Stanford Interests and Goals
prompts.prompt: Why Stanford? Describe your interest in studying at Stanford and how it relates to your passions and future goals. (450 characters maximum)
tips: Name specific programs, professors, or courses — avoid generic praise
Connect Stanford's unique features directly to your specific academic path
Show you've done real research, not just read the homepage
Link the location/resources to your specific goals
pitfalls: Using the same essay for multiple schools (admissions can tell)
Vague references to 'innovation' or ' Silicon Valley proximity' without depth
Focusing only on prestige rather than fit

prompts.title: MIT Meaningful Topic
prompts.prompt: Tell us about something that is meaningful to you, and why it is meaningful to you. (200-250 words)
tips: This is NOT the same as 'why MIT' — focus on meaning, not just intellectual interest
Choose something genuine to you, even if it's not academic
Use specific details to ground the abstract concept in reality
Let your values come through naturally via the story you choose
pitfalls: Writing this as a mini-Why MIT essay instead of a meaningful topic essay
Choosing something you think MIT wants to hear
Being so abstract that no one can picture what you mean

prompts.title: UChicago Intellectual Curiosity
prompts.prompt: UChicago's liberal arts approach encourages students to explore the world's problems with interdisciplinary thinking. How do you pursue your intellectual curiosity and what impact does this have on your community?
tips: Show genuine intellectual restlessness — the desire to connect disparate fields
Give concrete examples of how you've explored ideas beyond your school curriculum
Connect your thinking to real-world impact or community benefit
Let your quirky intellectual interests show — UChicago values authenticity
pitfalls: Listing subjects you like without showing how you connect them
Being pretentious about obscure interests
Ignoring the 'community impact' part of the question

prompts.title: Georgetown Challenge and Course of Study
prompts.prompt: Describe a significant challenge you have faced and/or a topic of importance to you that you feel could be addressed through your proposed course of study at Georgetown.
tips: Connect your personal challenge directly to Georgetown's specific academic strengths
Name specific departments, centers, or professors at Georgetown
Show how Georgetown is uniquely positioned for your solution
Make it clear this school has a distinct advantage for YOUR specific goals
pitfalls: Using a generic challenge that could apply to any school
Not naming anything specific about Georgetown's offerings
Confusing this with a general 'why this school' essay

prompts.title: Why STEM Major
prompts.prompt: Please describe your interest in your intended major in the School of Engineering or the sciences. What is it about studying and conducting research in your chosen field that most excites and sustains your curiosity?
tips: Get specific about sub-fields and research questions, not just broad STEM enthusiasm
Describe a specific moment of intellectual excitement or breakthrough
Connect your curiosity to what you want to DO with this knowledge
Show you understand the reality of research, not just the glamour
pitfalls: Saying you 'want to help people' without connecting to your specific scientific interest
Listing impressive-sounding fields without demonstrating real knowledge
Ignoring the 'sustains your curiosity' part — show long-term commitment

prompts.title: Why Humanities Major
prompts.prompt: What drives your fascination with the humanities, and how do you envision your studies in this field preparing you to address real-world challenges?
tips: Show that you understand what humanities actually study (not just 'I like reading')
Connect humanities skills to concrete career or life goals
Give an example of how you've already applied humanistic thinking
Avoid anti-science sentiment — show genuine interdisciplinary openness
pitfalls: Assuming humanities means 'anything not STEM'
Dismissing career applications as beneath you
Using vague phrases like 'understanding the human condition' without specifics

prompts.title: Why Business Major
prompts.prompt: Why are you drawn to studying business? Describe how you plan to use your business education to create value for your community, industry, or society.
tips: Get specific about WHAT kind of business — entrepreneurship, finance, social enterprise?
Show you understand business as creating and distributing value, not just making money
Connect your business interest to a genuine passion or problem you want to solve
Avoid only talking about personal success — emphasize value for others
pitfalls: Saying 'I've always wanted to be a CEO' without nuance
Ignoring the 'create value' part of the question
Assuming business is only about profit, not people or purpose

prompts.title: Why Interdisciplinary Studies
prompts.prompt: Describe your interest in an interdisciplinary area of study and how your proposed program connects to your academic and career goals.
tips: Be specific about the two or more fields you want to combine
Explain WHY these fields need each other — what's missing in siloed study
Show you understand the intellectual challenge of interdisciplinary work
Connect to a goal that actually requires this combination, not just 'I'm interested in everything'
pitfalls: Picking fields randomly because you can't decide
Not showing genuine depth in at least one of the fields
Assuming interdisciplinary is easier or a fallback option

prompts.title: Community Through Diversity
prompts.prompt: Our community is comprised of individuals from many different backgrounds, beliefs, and identities. How has your background shaped the way you engage with others, and what will you contribute to our community of scholars?
tips: Choose ONE specific aspect of your background rather than listing many
Show, don't tell: use a specific moment that illustrates your engagement style
Connect your background to how you'll contribute to academic dialogue
Avoid exoticizing your identity or making it the only interesting thing about you
pitfalls: Listing every identity category you belong to
Assuming your background is so unique no one else could understand
Writing about diversity as if you're observing it from outside, not living it

prompts.title: Community Contribution
prompts.prompt: Describe the community you come from and how your background has shaped your commitment to serving others. What specific community contribution would you make as a student at our institution?
tips: Name the specific community (neighborhood, religious group, cultural community) not just 'my community'
Show sustained commitment, not just one-time service
Connect service to your genuine skills and interests, not charity tourism
Be realistic about what you can contribute as a student, not just as a future alumnus
pitfalls: Confusing charity with community change or advocacy
Writing about helping 'others' as if you're not part of any community yourself
Promising unrealistic large-scale impact while a student

prompts.title: Background and Perspective
prompts.prompt: How has your background prepared you to contribute a unique perspective in your community and on our campus?
tips: Focus on perspective, not just background — what's the internal experience?
Show how your viewpoint creates opportunities for dialogue or collaboration
Be specific about what insight or skill you bring, not just 'diversity of thought'
Connect your perspective to genuine listening and learning, not just speaking
pitfalls: Treating your background as your only asset
Assuming perspective is only about identity, not experiences
Being so vague that any student could write this

prompts.title: Bridge Builder Between Communities
prompts.prompt: Describe an experience that positioned you as a bridge between different communities. What did this experience teach you about yourself and your ability to bring people together?
tips: Pick a specific moment when you had to navigate between two groups
Show both what you learned about others AND about yourself
Avoid claiming you've 'solved' division — show genuine complexity
Let the lessons be humbler and more honest than heroic
pitfalls: Acting as if you're a savior between communities
Not being specific about which communities and what the friction was
Claiming you've achieved reconciliation when you only had one conversation

prompts.title: Failure and Growth
prompts.prompt: Recount a time when you failed at something. What did you learn from this failure, and how did it change you?
tips: Choose a genuine failure, not a 'failure' that's secretly a success in disguise
Be honest about what you did wrong without being self-pitying
Show the specific behavioral or mindset change that resulted
The best failure essays make admissions officers like you MORE, not less
pitfalls: Describing a near-win as a failure to seem humble
Blaming others entirely for what went wrong
Claiming you learned something so general it could apply to anyone

prompts.title: Setback in Your Life
prompts.prompt: Describe a time when you experienced a significant setback. How did you respond, and what did this reveal about your character?
tips: Show your process of responding, not just the setback itself
Reveal character through your choices, not through self-description
Include what you could NOT control, but focus on what you could
Show resilience not as bravado but as honest struggle
pitfalls: Dramatizing a minor setback to seem dramatic
Claiming you 'never gave up' when the outcome was mixed
Writing about setbacks without any emotional authenticity

prompts.title: Overcoming an Obstacle
prompts.prompt: Tell us about an obstacle you encountered and how you overcame it. What resources, personal qualities, or circumstances helped you persist?
tips: Name the specific obstacle clearly — not 'challenges' in general
Identify what actually helped you persist (be specific about resources)
Show how this experience prepared you for future challenges
Let others' support be part of the story without diminishing your agency
pitfalls: Making it sound like you did it all alone (ignoring support systems)
Choosing an obstacle that's too small for the space
Claiming it was all about 'mindset' when structural barriers existed

prompts.title: Moral or Ethical Dilemma
prompts.prompt: Describe a situation in which you faced a difficult moral or ethical choice. How did you decide, and what were the consequences?
tips: Choose a situation with genuine moral complexity, not an obvious right/wrong
Show the competing values clearly before explaining your choice
Be honest about the imperfect nature of your decision
Include the consequences, not just the decision itself
pitfalls: Picking a situation where there's a clear hero/villain dynamic
Claiming you made the 'perfect' ethical choice
Being preachy about what you learned

prompts.title: An Idea That Fascinates
prompts.prompt: Share an idea, concept, or theory that you find genuinely fascinating. How did you encounter it, and what makes it so compelling to you?
tips: Get specific enough to show genuine depth of engagement
Show how you pursued this idea beyond what was required
Explain why it matters to you personally, not just intellectually
Let your excitement be contagious through specific details
pitfalls: Choosing an idea because it's 'smart' rather than because you love it
Being too technical for a non-specialist reader to follow
Summarizing the idea instead of showing your relationship to it

prompts.title: A Book That Changed Your Thinking
prompts.prompt: Tell us about a book, essay, or piece of writing that has influenced how you understand the world. What changed in your thinking after reading it?
tips: Choose a book that genuinely shaped you, not one you think sounds impressive
Show the before/after of your thinking, not just the book's content
Be honest if the book challenged you rather than simply enlightening you
Make clear this book matters to you personally, not just academically
pitfalls: Choosing a book everyone reads (Harry Potter, 1984) without adding your take
Writing a book report instead of an essay about YOUR change
Pretending you understood more than you did

prompts.title: Intellectual Curiosity
prompts.prompt: What is something you are genuinely curious about, and how do you pursue that curiosity? How has this curiosity shaped your intellectual journey?
tips: Choose a specific curiosity, not 'everything' or 'learning'
Show your process of exploring — what do you actually DO with curiosity
Connect curiosity to your academic path honestly
Let your curiosity be quirky or unexpected, not just academically impressive
pitfalls: Claiming to be curious about something you can't elaborate on
Equating curiosity with good grades or test scores
Being so broad ('I'm curious about everything!') that nothing shines through

prompts.title: Unconventional Topic Essay
prompts.prompt: Submit an essay on a topic of your choosing. This is your chance to be creative and share something that no other applicant will write in exactly this way.
tips: Choose a topic only YOU could write — your specific angle matters more than the topic itself
Use creative structure or voice intentionally, not randomly
Even unconventional essays need structure and purpose
Take a risk — the most memorable essays are often the bravest
pitfalls: Choosing weirdness for its own sake without substance
Forgetting that 'creative' still means clear writing
Being so experimental you lose the reader entirely

prompts.title: Unusual Angle on a Common Experience
prompts.prompt: Take a ordinary experience and write about it from an unexpected angle. Show us something familiar in a completely new way.
tips: Find the strange within the ordinary — choose a mundane topic with hidden depth
Your voice and observation skills matter more than the subject itself
Use precise, concrete details to make the familiar strange
Let the new angle reveal something meaningful, not just be clever
pitfalls: Choosing something so mundane you can't find the interesting angle
Forcing a metaphor or comparison that doesn't work
Being so focused on being clever you forget to mean something

prompts.title: Yale Short Answer - Activities
prompts.prompt: Briefly describe a short-term activity (2 weeks or less) that you've participated in recently. What made you decide to try this, and what did you get out of the experience?
tips: Choose something genuinely short-term that reveals something about you
Explain the decision to try it, not just what you did
Be specific about what you gained — insights, skills, perspectives
Short answers need efficient writing — every word must count
pitfalls: Stretching a two-week activity into something more impressive
Being vague about the activity or its impact
Using space to list rather than to reveal

prompts.title: UVA Short Answer - What Matters and Why
prompts.prompt: What is it that you love to do, and why does it matter to you? Why is this interest or passion meaningful to you?
tips: Be extremely specific about the activity or interest
Skip the 'why it matters' introduction — get into the heart of it quickly
Use concrete language, not abstract platitudes
Contradict yourself or show complexity if honest
pitfalls: Saying you love 'music' or 'sports' without specificity
Using the same generic answer every applicant would give
Wasting words on setup instead of diving in

prompts.title: Princeton Short Answer - Community Service Reflection
prompts.prompt: Princeton's motto is 'In the Nation's Service and the Service of Humanity.' How do you relate to this motto, and how might you personally contribute to it?
tips: Connect the motto to YOUR specific experiences and goals
Show what 'service' means to you specifically, not generically
Give a concrete example of how you've lived this motto
Avoid generic praise of service — show your personal stake
pitfalls: Rewording the motto without adding your own meaning
Making grandiose claims about your future service without grounding
Being so humble you don't claim any connection at all

prompts.title: Stanford Short Answer - Intellectual Engagement
prompts.prompt: The front page of your application is the first thing we will read. What five words best describe you, and why? (Note: choose five words that are not already used elsewhere in your application.)
tips: Choose words that are genuinely distinctive, not generic virtues
Each word needs justification through behavior or example
The 'why' is more important than the words themselves
Consider words that might seem contradictory — those can be interesting
pitfalls: Using adjectives any applicant might use (creative, hard-working, passionate)
Listing five words without explaining why they fit you
Using words that overlap with your activities or essays

prompts.title: Additional Information
prompts.prompt: Please provide any additional information you would like the admissions committee to consider as they review your application. This may include explanations of circumstances or context not captured elsewhere in your application.
tips: Only use this space if you genuinely have information that can't go elsewhere
Address specific gaps or circumstances directly and factually
Don't repeat information already in your application
End with something positive about your fit or potential, not just explanation
pitfalls: Using this to write a supplemental essay about your achievements
Making excuses for bad grades without taking responsibility
Assuming the committee doesn't read this section carefully
